text: Other than Ronaldo (and for the most part Casillas as well until his arm broke), every player in the squad was capable of being benched in any given match regardless of their fitness or form. Ramos was the top defender (at the time he could play both RB and CB) but he experienced plenty of rotation with Arbeloa, Pepe, Carvalho, Albiol, and eventually Varane. Mourinho loved Benzema, but still constantly rotated him with Higuain. Mourinho loved Ozil, but still constantly rotated him with Kaka. Marcelo frequently got rotated with Coentrao. Everyone remembers Alonso and Khedira as the main double pivot of the Mourinho era, but Lassana Diarra also started a ton of games back then. Mourinho specifically requested the signing of Modric, but even Modric was used rotationally (to the point that he was even famously named the worst signing of the season). At first I wasn’t sure whether the Bernardo signing made sense, but remembering all of this reminded me that it’s a perfectly sensible move. He’ll get big minutes, and it won’t necessarily have to be at the expense of other players’ big minutes as some people seem to be keen to speculate. Mourinho is going to operate a bit differently from the way Ancelotti or Zidane did. He doesn’t want people to be able to guess his lineup for any given match, because he isn’t here to build a lineup. He’s here to build a squad. Upvote 237 Downvote 50 Go to comments Share

text: Just a few weeks ago the mood around the club was terrible as we failed to win any major trophies, and at the same time, there were reports about Florentino looking at selling a minority stake in the club. After a season like this, you would think that would be a difficult conversation to have with the sócios. Then he called for elections, and for a few weeks the focus completely changed. Instead of everyone talking about the failure this season was, the conversation became about the election. Now Madrid’s season is exactly the same as it was a month ago, but Florentino seems to have come out of it with even more authority than before. People are talking about his win, and speculating who are the promised signings rather than the disappointing season we just had. It feels like he managed to turn a really bad situation into a positive for himself as he has more legitimacy and momentum, making it easier to move forward with projects that may have faced more resistance immediately after the season ended.

text: Random thought about the Olise to Madrid rumours… If Madrid really goes for him, could Nico Paz be added to the deal to make Bayern more open to selling? In the very unlikely scenario where Bayern actually sell Olise, Madrid buying Nico Paz back from Como could create the simplest solution: Madrid makes a huge bid for Olise, and Bayern uses part of that money to buy Nico Paz from Madrid as a cheaper, high-quality replacement with a similar technical profile. He’s obviously not Olise, but the profile is kind of similar: left-footed, creative, can play as a right-sided CAM/RW. For Bayern, they’d get a massive fee plus a young talented player who could help replace Olise. For Nico, the path to an important role in a top European squad would be pretty clear as a CAM or RW, with Gnabry, Lennart Karl and Musiala as competition. That honestly feels like a better situation than coming back to a Real Madrid side in crisis, with an unclear playing idea and not much status compared to the other attackers. Not saying it’s likely, but it could be a smart way to make the deal easier for Bayern.
